context.pr_title: Fix issue with > in pip cmd causing file creation
context.pr_body:
### Problem Summary

When starting the Web UI, the `install.py` script reads `requirements.txt`. If a package version uses ">", it is treated as a redirection in `pip install`, creating an unintended file.

### Steps to Reproduce

Running `webui.bat` or `webui.sh` creates a file named "=4.8.0" due to "opencv-python>=4.8.0" in `requirements.txt` being misinterpreted.

### Fix

I enclosed the package and version specification in double quotes in `install.py`.

agents.name: analysis_agent
agents.system_prompt:
You are an Analysis Agent specialized in Pull Request analysis.

## Project Context (MASCA Analysis)
## Project Analysis

### README
# ControlNet for Stable Diffusion WebUI

The WebUI extension for ControlNet and other injection-based SD controls.
![image](https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/assets/20929282/261f9a50-ba9c-472f-b398-fced61929c4a)

This extension is for AUTOMATIC1111's [Stable Diffusion web UI](https://github.com/AUTOMATIC1111/stable-diffusion-webui), allows the Web UI to add [ControlNet](https://github.com/lllyasviel/ControlNet) to the original Stable Diffusion model to generate images. The addition is on-the-fly, the merging is not required.

# News

- [2024-05-19] 🔥[v1.1.449] Anyline Preprocessor & MistoLine SDXL model [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2907]
- [2024-05-04] 🔥[v1.1.447] PuLID [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2841]
- [2024-04-30] 🔥[v1.1.446] Effective region mask supported for ControlNet/IPAdapter [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2831]
- [2024-04-27] 🔥ControlNet-lllite Normal Dsine released [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2813]
- [2024-04-19] 🔥[v1.1.445] IPAdapter advanced weight [Instant Style] [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2770]
- [2024-04-17] 🔥[v1.1.444] Marigold depth preprocessor [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2760]
- [2024-04-15] 🔥ControlNet++ models released [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2778]
- [2024-04-13] 🔥TTPLanet_SDXL_Controlnet_Tile_Realistic v2 released [[Civitai Page](https://civitai.com/models/330313/ttplanetsdxlcontrolnettilerealistic)]
- [2024-03-31] 🔥[v1.1.443] IP-Adapter CLIP mask and ip-adapter-auto preprocessor [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2723]
- [2024-03-20] 🔥IPAdapter Composition [Discussion thread: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/discussions/2781]

# Installation

1. Open "Extensions" tab.
2. Open "Install from URL" tab in the tab.
3. Enter `https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet.git` to "URL for extension's git repository".
4. Press "Install" button.
5. Wait for 5 seconds, and you will see the message "Installed into stable-diffusion-webui\extensions\sd-webui-controlnet. Use Installed tab to restart".
6. Go to "Installed" tab, click "Check for updates", and then click "Apply and restart UI". (The next time you can also use these buttons to update ControlNet.)
7. Completely restart A1111 webui including your terminal. (If you do not know what is a "terminal", you can reboot your computer to achieve the same effect.)
8. Download models (see below).
9. After you put models in the correct folder, you may need to refresh to see the models. The refresh button is right to your "Model" dropdown.

# Download Models
You can find all download links here: https://github.com/Mikubill/sd-webui-controlnet/wiki/Model-d


## Your Task
When you receive a PR title and body, you must:

1. **Analyze the request**: Understand what the PR wants to achieve
2. **Explore the code**: Use tools to navigate base_project and find relevant files/functions
3. **Identify targets**: Determine exactly which files and functions need to be modified
4. **Optimize MASCA**: Produce a version of the MASCA analysis focused on the specific PR

## Available Tools
- `read_base_project_file(file_path)`: Read a file from the project
- `list_base_project_directory(directory, pattern, recursive)`: List files/folders

## Required Output
You must produce a structured output with:
- `pr_title`: PR title
- `pr_body`: PR body
- `masca_optimized`: MASCA optimized for this specific PR
- `files_to_modify`: List of files to modify with motivation
- `functions_to_modify`: List of functions to modify with motivation
- `analysis_summary`: Summary of your analysis

## Guidelines
- Explore the code before drawing conclusions
- Search for files with names relevant to the PR
- Identify specific functions, not just generic files
- Be precise with paths (relative to base_project)
- For function names use the format "ClassName.method_name" for methods
agents.input_prompt:
Analyze this Pull Request:

## Title
Fix issue with > in pip cmd causing file creation

## Description
### Problem Summary

When starting the Web UI, the `install.py` script reads `requirements.txt`. If a package version uses ">", it is treated as a redirection in `pip install`, creating an unintended file.

### Steps to Reproduce

Running `webui.bat` or `webui.sh` creates a file named "=4.8.0" due to "opencv-python>=4.8.0" in `requirements.txt` being misinterpreted.

### Fix

I enclosed the package and version specification in double quotes in `install.py`.

Explore the source code to identify the files and functions to modify.

output.pr_title: Fix issue with > in pip cmd causing file creation
output.pr_body:
### Problem Summary

When starting the Web UI, the `install.py` script reads `requirements.txt`. If a package version uses ">", it is treated as a redirection in `pip install`, creating an unintended file.

### Steps to Reproduce

Running `webui.bat` or `webui.sh` creates a file named "=4.8.0" due to "opencv-python>=4.8.0" in `requirements.txt` being misinterpreted.

### Fix

I enclosed the package and version specification in double quotes in `install.py`.
output.masca_optimized:
Summary of intent
- Problem: When install.py constructs pip commands like `pip install opencv-python>=4.8.0`, the `>` is interpreted by the shell (especially on Windows) as redirection, creating files like `=4.8.0`.
- Goal: Ensure pip arguments that include comparison operators (>, >=, <=, ==) or URLs are passed to pip as a single quoted argument so the shell doesn't split/interprete them.

Files & functions to inspect
- install.py: single source of pip invocation logic in this repository. Inspect all launch.run_pip(...) calls and ensure the pip target argument is quoted.

Exact changes required (high-level)
- Wrap package specification strings in double quotes in all launch.run_pip calls in install.py. Examples:
  - change f"install -U {package}" -> f'install -U "{package}"'
  - change f"install {package}" -> f'install "{package}"'
  - change f"install {wheel_url}" -> f'install "{wheel_url}"'
  - change f"install -U {wheel_url}" -> f'install -U "{wheel_url}"'

Why these modifications
- Quoting prevents the shell from interpreting characters like '>' as redirection. It is the minimal, safe change that preserves behavior while avoiding unintended file creation.

Edgecases and notes
- Quoting package names or wheel URLs is harmless for pip; pip accepts quoted arguments.
- The checks that split package strings by "==", ">=", "<=" remain valid and unaffected.
- Make sure to update all run_pip calls in install.py (including ones inside try_install_from_wheel and try_install_insight_face).

Suggested tests
- Start the UI with a requirements entry like "opencv-python>=4.8.0" and confirm no file named "=4.8.0" is created and pip is invoked properly.
- Confirm on Windows cmd and Unix shells.

files_to_modify.reason: This file constructs and invokes pip commands (launch.run_pip). The issue originates here: package strings like 'opencv-python>=4.8.0' are interpolated into shell commands and the '>' is treated as shell redirection. Quoting the package arguments in all pip invocations in this file prevents that behavior.

functions_to_modify.reason: This function reads requirements.txt and calls launch.run_pip with interpolated package strings in multiple branches (==, >=, <=, and plain install). These invocations must quote the package argument to avoid shell redirection of '>' characters.

This function constructs a pip install command for 'onnxruntime' or 'onnxruntime-gpu' and calls launch.run_pip. Quoting the package name prevents any potential shell parsing issues and keeps consistency.
This function calls launch.run_pip with wheel URLs (f"install -U {wheel_url}"). Wrapping the URL in quotes avoids shell misinterpretation (and is consistent with other fixes).
This function calls launch.run_pip(f"install {wheel_url}", ...). The wheel_url should be quoted to prevent shell parsing issues, and to match the other run_pip changes.

output.analysis_summary: The bug is caused by unquoted pip arguments that include comparison operators (e.g., '>='). The only file that constructs such pip commands in this repo is install.py. Fixing it requires quoting the package / URL argument in every launch.run_pip call inside install.py (install_requirements, install_onnxruntime, try_install_from_wheel, try_install_insight_face). This prevents shells from interpreting '>' as redirection and avoids accidental file creation like '=4.8.0'. The change is minimal and safe (pip accepts quoted arguments). Test by running the UI with requirements containing 'opencv-python>=4.8.0' on Windows and Unix shells.

agents.name: context_planner_agent
agents.system_prompt:
You are a Context Planner Agent specialized in step plan generation.

## Your Task
You receive from the Analysis Agent:
- PR title and body
- Optimized MASCA
- List of files to modify
- List of functions to modify

You must:
1. **Retrieve context**: Use tools to read the context files of the identified functions
2. **Analyze dependencies**: Understand how the functions interact
3. **Generate step plan**: Create a detailed step-by-step plan

## Available Tools
- `read_context_file(file_path)`: Read a pre-generated context file
- `list_context_files(directory, pattern)`: List available context files
- `read_call_graph(section)`: Read information from the call graph

## Required Output
You must produce a `step_plan` with:
- `summary`: 1-2 sentence summary of what the PR does
- `steps`: Ordered list of steps, each with:
  - `operation`: What to do (e.g., "Add validation method")
  - `file_to_modify`: Relative path of the file to modify (e.g., "src/module.py")
  - `function_to_modify`: Name of the function to modify (e.g., "ClassName.method_name")
  - `reason`: Technical motivation
  - `side_effects`: Potential impacts

## IMPORTANT RULE for function_to_modify
- If the file to modify is a Python file (.py), specify the function/method to modify
- If the file is NOT a Python file (e.g., .js, .html, .css, .json, .yaml, .md, etc.),
  the value of `function_to_modify` MUST be null

## Guidelines for Steps
- Each step must be atomic and implementable
- The order of steps must be logical (dependencies first)
- For Python files: always specify the function in the format "FunctionName" or "ClassName.method_name"
- For non-Python files: function_to_modify must be null
- The side_effects should mention tests, dependents, and API impacts
- You MUST generate a number of step EQUAL to the number of the python functions you are intendeed to modify nomore neither less

## Step Examples

### For Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Add time_spanned_alpha method",
  "file_to_modify": "manimlib/animation/animation.py",
  "function_to_modify": "Animation.time_spanned_alpha",
  "reason": "To compute animation progress based on actual elapsed time",
  "side_effects": "May require downstream animations to call this method"
}
```

### For non-Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Update API endpoint configuration",
  "file_to_modify": "config/routes.yaml",
  "function_to_modify": null,
  "reason": "Add new route for the authentication endpoint",
  "side_effects": "Requires server restart to apply changes"
}
```
agents.input_prompt:
Generate an implementation plan for this PR:

## PR Title
Fix issue with > in pip cmd causing file creation

## PR Body
### Problem Summary

When starting the Web UI, the `install.py` script reads `requirements.txt`. If a package version uses ">", it is treated as a redirection in `pip install`, creating an unintended file.

### Steps to Reproduce

Running `webui.bat` or `webui.sh` creates a file named "=4.8.0" due to "opencv-python>=4.8.0" in `requirements.txt` being misinterpreted.

### Fix

I enclosed the package and version specification in double quotes in `install.py`.

## MASCA Analysis (optimized)
Summary of intent
- Problem: When install.py constructs pip commands like `pip install opencv-python>=4.8.0`, the `>` is interpreted by the shell (especially on Windows) as redirection, creating files like `=4.8.0`.
- Goal: Ensure pip arguments that include comparison operators (>, >=, <=, ==) or URLs are passed to pip as a single quoted argument so the shell doesn't split/interprete them.

Files & functions to inspect
- install.py: single source of pip invocation logic in this repository. Inspect all launch.run_pip(...) calls and ensure the pip target argument is quoted.

Exact changes required (high-level)
- Wrap package specification strings in double quotes in all launch.run_pip calls in install.py. Examples:
  - change f"install -U {package}" -> f'install -U "{package}"'
  - change f"install {package}" -> f'install "{package}"'
  - change f"install {wheel_url}" -> f'install "{wheel_url}"'
  - change f"install -U {wheel_url}" -> f'install -U "{wheel_url}"'

Why these modifications
- Quoting prevents the shell from interpreting characters like '>' as redirection. It is the minimal, safe change that preserves behavior while avoiding unintended file creation.

Edgecases and notes
- Quoting package names or wheel URLs is harmless for pip; pip accepts quoted arguments.
- The checks that split package strings by "==", ">=", "<=" remain valid and unaffected.
- Make sure to update all run_pip calls in install.py (including ones inside try_install_from_wheel and try_install_insight_face).

Suggested tests
- Start the UI with a requirements entry like "opencv-python>=4.8.0" and confirm no file named "=4.8.0" is created and pip is invoked properly.
- Confirm on Windows cmd and Unix shells.


## Files to Modify
- install.py: This file constructs and invokes pip commands (launch.run_pip). The issue originates here: package strings like 'opencv-python>=4.8.0' are interpolated into shell commands and the '>' is treated as shell redirection. Quoting the package arguments in all pip invocations in this file prevents that behavior.

## Functions to Modify
- install_requirements (install.py): This function reads requirements.txt and calls launch.run_pip with interpolated package strings in multiple branches (==, >=, <=, and plain install). These invocations must quote the package argument to avoid shell redirection of '>' characters.
- install_onnxruntime (install.py): This function constructs a pip install command for 'onnxruntime' or 'onnxruntime-gpu' and calls launch.run_pip. Quoting the package name prevents any potential shell parsing issues and keeps consistency.
- try_install_from_wheel (install.py): This function calls launch.run_pip with wheel URLs (f"install -U {wheel_url}"). Wrapping the URL in quotes avoids shell misinterpretation (and is consistent with other fixes).
- try_install_insight_face (install.py): This function calls launch.run_pip(f"install {wheel_url}", ...). The wheel_url should be quoted to prevent shell parsing issues, and to match the other run_pip changes.

## Analysis Summary
The bug is caused by unquoted pip arguments that include comparison operators (e.g., '>='). The only file that constructs such pip commands in this repo is install.py. Fixing it requires quoting the package / URL argument in every launch.run_pip call inside install.py (install_requirements, install_onnxruntime, try_install_from_wheel, try_install_insight_face). This prevents shells from interpreting '>' as redirection and avoids accidental file creation like '=4.8.0'. The change is minimal and safe (pip accepts quoted arguments). Test by running the UI with requirements containing 'opencv-python>=4.8.0' on Windows and Unix shells.

Use the context files to understand dependencies and generate a detailed step-by-step plan.

steps.operation: Quote wheel_url argument in pip invocation
steps.reason: This helper constructs pip commands using wheel URLs (e.g., f"install -U {wheel_url}"). Unquoted URLs containing characters interpreted by the shell (like '>') can be mis-parsed; wrapping the URL in double quotes (f'install -U "{wheel_url}"') prevents shell interpretation and ensures pip receives the entire argument.
steps.side_effects: No change to pip behavior; safer cross-shell handling. Tests that validate exact subprocess invocation strings may need updating. Downstream callers of try_install_from_wheel are unaffected except that their invoked pip subprocess receives a quoted argument string. No API changes.

steps.operation: Quote wheel_url argument in pip invocation
steps.reason: This function calls launch.run_pip with a wheel URL (e.g., f"install {wheel_url}"). Wrapping the wheel URL in double quotes (f'install "{wheel_url}"') prevents shells from interpreting special characters and matches the quoting used elsewhere.
steps.side_effects: No functional change to package installation. Tests that assert the exact command string passed to launch.run_pip might need to be updated to expect the quoted argument. Ensures consistent behavior across Windows and Unix shells.

steps.operation: Quote package name in onnxruntime pip install command
steps.reason: install_onnxruntime builds pip install commands for 'onnxruntime' or 'onnxruntime-gpu' (e.g., f"install {onnx_pkg}"). Quoting the package (f'install "{onnx_pkg}"') ensures that any accidental special characters are not interpreted by the shell and keeps behavior consistent with other fixes.
steps.side_effects: No semantic change; safer across shells. If tests parse the unquoted command string, update them to accept or expect the quoted form. No change to the public API; only affects the exact subprocess command string.

steps.operation: Quote package/version specifiers in all pip invocations
steps.reason: install_requirements reads requirements.txt and builds pip commands for different spec forms (==, >=, <=, or plain installs). Changing interpolations from f"install -U {package}" to f'install -U "{package}"' (and same for other forms and wheel URLs) prevents shell redirection caused by unescaped '>' characters (e.g., 'opencv-python>=4.8.0'), eliminating unintended file creation.
steps.side_effects: Prevents accidental file creation from shell redirection on Windows and Unix. Pip accepts quoted arguments so installation behavior remains unchanged. Tests that validate exact subprocess strings should be updated. Ensure that any code that parses the pip command string still works with quotes; dependent tooling that expects unquoted tokens may require small adjustments.

step_plan.summary: Quote pip target arguments in all launch.run_pip calls within install.py to prevent shell interpretation of characters like '>' (which caused files like '=4.8.0' to be created). The change wraps package names and wheel URLs in double quotes across install_requirements, install_onnxruntime, try_install_from_wheel, and try_install_insight_face.
